moveAnnotations.annotation: We're opening with the king's pawn two squares forward, claiming the center and freeing both our bishop and queen for future development. This classical first move immediately puts pressure on the d5 and f5 squares while opening lines for our pieces. The move 1.e4 leads to sharp, tactical games and is favored by players who enjoy direct attacking chances. Our plan is to quickly develop our pieces, castle our king to safety, and look for opportunities to exploit Black's potentially irregular development if they choose an offbeat defense.

Our opponent chooses the unconventional Nimzowitsch Defense, developing the queen's knight before moving any pawns. This hypermodern approach deliberately avoids occupying the center with pawns, instead planning to attack it from the flanks with pieces. While this move does develop a piece and attacks our e4 pawn, it blocks the c-pawn and doesn't contribute to Black's central control as effectively as the standard 1...e5 or 1...c5. We should respond by continuing our natural development while maintaining our central advantage, as Black's setup can become cramped if we develop harmoniously.

We're developing our bishop to an active square where it pins the knight to the black king, creating immediate tactical pressure. This move characterizes the Pseudo-Spanish Variation of the Nimzowitsch Defense, as we're employing the Spanish Bishop setup despite Black's irregular knight development. The bishop on b5 controls important central squares like d7 and c6, while also preparing to potentially trade the knight that's attacking our e4 pawn. This natural developing move maintains our opening advantage by forcing Black to deal with the pin, and we're ready to castle kingside quickly while keeping pressure on Black's somewhat awkward piece coordination.
